revision.comment: 中世日本宗教史上の用語（とりあえず版）
revision.text:
'''顕密体制'''（けんみつたいせい）とは、歴史学者[[黒田俊雄]]が提唱した[[日本]]の[[中世]]宗教体制に関する学説。[[岩波書店]]『日本中世の国家と宗教』（[[1975年]]）で「中世における顕密体制の展開」というタイトルで発表した。
== 概略 == 戦後の中世仏教史では、旧仏教である[[南都六宗]]と[[平安二宗]]（[[天台宗]]、[[真言宗]]）は対等な存在として対立的または排他的な関係のもとに併立しているとの見方が大勢を占めていた。
それに対し黒田は、文献に現れる[[顕密]]（当時は一切の仏教を表現する用語）という言葉を用語化して顕密体制論という学説を提唱した。
実際に日本中世において正統と見なされた宗教は、[[平安時代]]以来[[密教]]を基軸に統合された顕密の仏教であり、旧仏教八宗は併立していたのではなく、密教に見ることが出来る鎮魂呪術的信仰という共通の基盤の上に密教の絶対的・普遍的真実性を前提とした競合的な秩序を形成していたものであるとし、[[延暦寺]]などの旧仏教系寺院が、このような秩序を保ちつつ国家権力と密着し、正統な宗教としてのあり方を固めた体制が中世を通じて見られたとするのが顕密体制論である。
この理論により、戦後見られた鎌倉新仏教を中世仏教の代表と見なす見解に対し、旧仏教が中世仏教の本流であるという認識が生まれた。また、黒田の提唱した[[権門体制論]]の国家像を前提としながら、政治社会史全体の構図の中に仏教を取り入れることにより、仏教史に新たな視点を追加した。
[[Category:日本史|けんみつたいせい]] {{Japanese-history-stub|けんみつたいせい}}

この理論により、戦後見られた鎌倉新仏教を中世仏教の代表と見なす見解に対し、旧仏教が中世仏教の本流であるという認識が生まれた。また、黒田の提唱した[[権門体制論]]の国家像を前提としながら、政治社会史全体の構図の中に仏教を取り入れることにより、仏教史に新たな視点を追加した。

'''Kenmitsu System''' (顕密体制) is a theory regarding the medieval [[Japan]]ese [[religion]] system proposed by historian [[Toshio Kuroda]] (黒田俊雄). It was published under the title "The Development of the Kenmitsu System in the Middle Ages" in [[Iwanami Shoten]]'s *The State and Religion in Medieval Japan* (1975).
== Overview ==
In postwar studies of medieval [[Buddhist history]], the prevailing view was that the old Buddhist schools, the [[Six Nanto Schools]] and the [[Two Heian Schools]] ({{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}}, {{ill|Shingon|ja|真言宗}}), coexisted in a confrontational or exclusive relationship as equal entities.
In contrast, Kuroda formulated the term [[Kenmitsu]] (which at the time referred to all Buddhism) and proposed the theory of the Kenmitsu System.
In reality, the religion regarded as orthodox in medieval Japan was the Kenmitsu Buddhism, which had been unified around {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}} since the [[Heian period]]. The eight old Buddhist schools did not merely coexist but rather formed a competitive order based on a shared foundation of esoteric Buddhist beliefs, particularly the thaumaturgic and spirit-pacifying aspects of Esoteric Buddhism, which was accepted as absolute and universal truth. Kuroda argued that institutions such as {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}}, part of the old Buddhist system, maintained this order while remaining closely tied to [[state]] [[power]], thereby solidifying their position as the orthodox religious establishment throughout the medieval period.
This theory led to a shift in postwar perspectives that had considered [[Kamakura New Buddhism]] as representative of medieval Buddhism. Instead, it fostered the recognition that old Buddhism was the mainstream of medieval Buddhism. Furthermore, by incorporating Buddhism into the overall framework of political and social history, based on Kuroda's proposed {{ill|Kenmon System Theory|ja|権門体制論}}, it introduced a new perspective to Buddhist historiography.
== See also == * {{ill|Temple and Shrine Powers|ja|寺社勢力}}

'''Kenmitsu System''' (顕密体制) is a theory regarding the medieval [[Japan]]ese [[religion]] system proposed by historian [[Toshio Kuroda]] (黒田俊雄). It was published under the title "The Development of the Kenmitsu System in the Middle Ages" in [[Iwanami Shoten]]'s *The State and Religion in Medieval Japan* (1975).
== Overview ==
In postwar studies of medieval [[Buddhist history]], the prevailing view was that the old Buddhist schools, the [[Six Nanto Schools]] and the [[Two Heian Schools]] ({{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}}, {{ill|Shingon|ja|真言宗}}), coexisted in a confrontational or exclusive relationship as equal entities.
In contrast, Kuroda formulated the term [[Kenmitsu]] (which at the time referred to all Buddhism) and proposed the theory of the Kenmitsu System.
In reality, the religion regarded as orthodox in medieval Japan was the Kenmitsu Buddhism, which had been unified around {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}} since the [[Heian period]]. The eight old Buddhist schools did not merely coexist but rather formed a competitive order based on a shared foundation of esoteric Buddhist beliefs, particularly the thaumaturgic and spirit-pacifying aspects of Esoteric Buddhism, which was accepted as absolute and universal truth. Kuroda argued that institutions such as {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}}, part of the old Buddhist system, maintained this order while remaining closely tied to [[state]] [[power]], thereby solidifying their position as the orthodox religious establishment throughout the medieval period.
This theory led to a shift in postwar perspectives that had considered [[Kamakura New Buddhism]] as representative of medieval Buddhism. Instead, it fostered the recognition that old Buddhism was the mainstream of medieval Buddhism. Furthermore, by incorporating Buddhism into the overall framework of political and social history, based on Kuroda's proposed {{ill|Kenmon System Theory|ja|権門体制論}}, it introduced a new perspective to Buddhist historiography.

'''Kenmitsu System''' (顕密体制) is a theory regarding the medieval [[Japan]]ese [[religion]] system proposed by historian [[Toshio Kuroda]] (黒田俊雄). It was published under the title "The Development of the Kenmitsu System in the Middle Ages" in [[Iwanami Shoten]]'s *The State and Religion in Medieval Japan* (1975).
'''Kenmitsu System''' (顕密体制, Kenmitsu Taisei) is a theory proposed by historian {{ill|Toshio Kuroda|ja|黒田俊雄}} regarding the religious system of medieval {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. It was introduced in the book *The Medieval Japanese State and Religion* (『日本中世の国家と宗教』), published by {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}} in [[1975]], under the title *The Development of the Kenmitsu System in the Middle Ages*.
== Overview == In postwar studies of medieval {{ill|Buddhist history of Japan|ja|日本仏教史}}, the dominant view was that the old Buddhist schools, the {{ill|Six Nanto Schools|ja|南都六宗}} and the {{ill|Two Heian Schools|ja|平安二宗}} ({{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} and {{ill|Shingon|ja|真言宗}}), existed in a **mutually exclusive or oppositional** relationship.
In contrast, Kuroda introduced the term **Kenmitsu (顕密)**—which at the time referred to all of Buddhism—as a conceptual framework and proposed the **Kenmitsu System Theory (顕密体制論)**.
According to Kuroda, the **orthodox religion** in medieval Japan was **Kenmitsu Buddhism**, which had been integrated **around Esoteric Buddhism** (密教) since the **Heian period**. The Eight Traditional Buddhist Schools were **not separate entities** but functioned within **a competitive yet unified order**, based on **a common foundation of esoteric rituals and mystical faiths focused on pacification and incantations**. Within this system, Esoteric Buddhism (密教) was considered the **absolute and universal truth**. Temples of the **old Buddhist schools**, such as {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}}, maintained **close ties to state power**, solidifying their position as **the legitimate religious institutions** throughout the medieval period. This state of affairs is what Kuroda termed the **Kenmitsu System**.
This theory challenged the **postwar perception** that saw the **Kamakura New Buddhism (鎌倉新仏教)** as the **main representative of medieval Buddhism**, instead emphasizing that **the old Buddhist schools were the true mainstream of medieval Japanese Buddhism**. Additionally, by incorporating **Buddhism into the broader framework of political and social history**, Kuroda's theory—built upon his earlier **Kenmon System Theory (権門体制論)**—introduced **a new perspective on Buddhist history**.

In reality, the religion regarded as orthodox in medieval Japan was the Kenmitsu Buddhism, which had been unified around {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}} since the [[Heian period]]. The eight old Buddhist schools did not merely coexist but rather formed a competitive order based on a shared foundation of esoteric Buddhist beliefs, particularly the thaumaturgic and spirit-pacifying aspects of Esoteric Buddhism, which was accepted as absolute and universal truth. Kuroda argued that institutions such as {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}}, part of the old Buddhist system, maintained this order while remaining closely tied to [[state]] [[power]], thereby solidifying their position as the orthodox religious establishment throughout the medieval period.

'''Kenmitsu System''' (顕密体制, Kenmitsu Taisei) is a theory proposed by historian {{ill|Toshio Kuroda|ja|黒田俊雄}} regarding the religious system of medieval {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. It was introduced in the book *The Medieval Japanese State and Religion* (『日本中世の国家と宗教』), published by {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}} in [[1975]], under the title *The Development of the Kenmitsu System in the Middle Ages*.
== Overview == In postwar studies of medieval {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|ja|日本仏教史|Buddhism in Japan=Buddhism in Japan|lt=Buddhist history of Japan}}, the dominant view was that the old Buddhist schools, the {{ill|Nanto Rokushū|ja|南都六宗|Nanto Rokushū=Nanto Rokushū|lt=Six Nanto Schools}} and the {{ill|Two Heian Schools|ja|平安二宗}} ({{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} and {{ill|Shingon|ja|真言宗}}), existed in a '''mutually exclusive or oppositional''' relationship.
In contrast, Kuroda introduced the term '''Kenmitsu (顕密)'''—which at the time referred to all of Buddhism—as a conceptual framework and proposed the '''Kenmitsu System Theory (顕密体制論)'''.
According to Kuroda, the '''orthodox religion''' in medieval Japan was '''Kenmitsu Buddhism''', which had been integrated '''around {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}''' (密教) since the '''[[Heian period]]'''. The Eight Traditional Buddhist Schools were '''not separate entities''' but functioned within '''a competitive yet unified order''', based on '''a common foundation of esoteric rituals and mystical faiths focused on pacification and incantations'''. Within this system, Esoteric Buddhism (密教) was considered the '''absolute and universal truth'''. Temples of the '''old Buddhist schools''', such as {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}}, maintained '''close ties to state power''', solidifying their position as '''the legitimate religious institutions''' throughout the medieval period. This state of affairs is what Kuroda termed the '''Kenmitsu System'''.
This theory challenged the '''postwar perception''' that saw the {{ill|Kamakura New Buddhism|ja|鎌倉新仏教}} '''(鎌倉新仏教)''' as the '''main representative of medieval Buddhism''', instead emphasizing that '''the old Buddhist schools were the true mainstream of medieval Japanese Buddhism'''. Additionally, by incorporating '''Buddhism into the broader framework of political and social history''', Kuroda's theory—built upon his earlier '''{{ill|Kenmon System Theory|ja|権門体制論}} (権門体制論)'''—introduced '''a new perspective on Buddhist history'''.
== See Also ==

revision.text:
'''Kenmitsu System''' (顕密体制, Kenmitsu Taisei) is a theory proposed by historian {{ill|Toshio Kuroda|ja|黒田俊雄}} regarding the religious system of medieval {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. It was introduced in the book *The Medieval Japanese State and Religion* (『日本中世の国家と宗教』), published by {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}} in [[1975]], under the title *The Development of the Kenmitsu System in the Middle Ages*.
== Overview == In postwar studies of medieval {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|ja|日本仏教史|Buddhism in Japan=Buddhism in Japan|lt=Buddhist history of Japan}}, the dominant view was that the old Buddhist schools, the {{ill|Nanto Rokushū|ja|南都六宗|Nanto Rokushū=Nanto Rokushū|lt=Six Nanto Schools}} and the {{ill|Two Heian Schools|ja|平安二宗}} ({{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} and {{ill|Shingon|ja|真言宗}}), existed in a '''mutually exclusive or oppositional''' relationship.
In contrast, Kuroda introduced the term '''Kenmitsu (顕密)'''—which at the time referred to all of Buddhism—as a conceptual framework and proposed the '''Kenmitsu System Theory (顕密体制論)'''.
According to Kuroda, the '''orthodox religion''' in medieval Japan was '''Kenmitsu Buddhism''', which had been integrated '''around {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}''' (密教) since the '''[[Heian period]]'''. The Eight Traditional Buddhist Schools were '''not separate entities''' but functioned within '''a competitive yet unified order''', based on '''a common foundation of esoteric rituals and mystical faiths focused on pacification and incantations'''. Within this system, Esoteric Buddhism (密教) was considered the '''absolute and universal truth'''. Temples of the '''old Buddhist schools''', such as {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}}, maintained '''close ties to state power''', solidifying their position as '''the legitimate religious institutions''' throughout the medieval period. This state of affairs is what Kuroda termed the '''Kenmitsu System'''.
This theory challenged the '''postwar perception''' that saw the {{ill|Kamakura New Buddhism|ja|鎌倉新仏教}} '''(鎌倉新仏教)''' as the '''main representative of medieval Buddhism''', instead emphasizing that '''the old Buddhist schools were the true mainstream of medieval Japanese Buddhism'''. Additionally, by incorporating '''Buddhism into the broader framework of political and social history''', Kuroda's theory—built upon his earlier '''{{ill|Kenmon System Theory|ja|権門体制論}} (権門体制論)'''—introduced '''a new perspective on Buddhist history'''.
== See Also ==

'''Kenmitsu System''' (顕密体制, Kenmitsu Taisei) is a theory proposed by historian {{ill|Toshio Kuroda|ja|黒田俊雄}} regarding the religious system of medieval {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. It was introduced in the book *The Medieval Japanese State and Religion* (『日本中世の国家と宗教』), published by {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}} in [[1975]], under the title *The Development of the Kenmitsu System in the Middle Ages*.
== Overview == In postwar studies of medieval {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|ja|日本仏教史|Buddhism in Japan=Buddhism in Japan|lt=Buddhist history of Japan}}, the dominant view was that the old Buddhist schools, the {{ill|Nanto Rokushū|ja|南都六宗|Nanto Rokushū=Nanto Rokushū|lt=Six Nanto Schools}} and the {{ill|Two Heian Schools|ja|平安二宗}} ({{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} and {{ill|Shingon|ja|真言宗}}), existed in a '''mutually exclusive or oppositional''' relationship.
In contrast, Kuroda introduced the term '''Kenmitsu (顕密)'''—which at the time referred to all of Buddhism—as a conceptual framework and proposed the '''Kenmitsu System Theory (顕密体制論)'''.
According to Kuroda, the '''orthodox religion''' in medieval Japan was '''Kenmitsu Buddhism''', which had been integrated '''around {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}''' (密教) since the '''[[Heian period]]'''. The Eight Traditional Buddhist Schools were '''not separate entities''' but functioned within '''a competitive yet unified order''', based on '''a common foundation of esoteric rituals and mystical faiths focused on pacification and incantations'''. Within this system, Esoteric Buddhism (密教) was considered the '''absolute and universal truth'''. Temples of the '''old Buddhist schools''', such as {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}}, maintained '''close ties to state power''', solidifying their position as '''the legitimate religious institutions''' throughout the medieval period. This state of affairs is what Kuroda termed the '''Kenmitsu System'''.
This theory challenged the '''postwar perception''' that saw the {{ill|Kamakura New Buddhism|ja|鎌倉新仏教}} '''(鎌倉新仏教)''' as the '''main representative of medieval Buddhism''', instead emphasizing that '''the old Buddhist schools were the true mainstream of medieval Japanese Buddhism'''. Additionally, by incorporating '''Buddhism into the broader framework of political and social history''', Kuroda's theory—built upon his earlier '''{{ill|Kenmon System Theory|ja|権門体制論}} (権門体制論)'''—introduced '''a new perspective on Buddhist history'''.
== See Also ==

This theory challenged the '''postwar perception''' that saw the {{ill|Kamakura New Buddhism|ja|鎌倉新仏教}} '''(鎌倉新仏教)''' as the '''main representative of medieval Buddhism''', instead emphasizing that '''the old Buddhist schools were the true mainstream of medieval Japanese Buddhism'''. Additionally, by incorporating '''Buddhism into the broader framework of political and social history''', Kuroda's theory—built upon his earlier '''{{ill|Kenmon System Theory|ja|権門体制論}} (権門体制論)'''—introduced '''a new perspective on Buddhist history'''.

'''Kenmitsu System''' (顕密体制, Kenmitsu Taisei) is a theory proposed by historian {{ill|Toshio Kuroda|ja|黒田俊雄}} regarding the religious system of medieval {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. It was introduced in the book *The Medieval Japanese State and Religion* (『日本中世の国家と宗教』), published by {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}} in [[1975]], under the title *The Development of the Kenmitsu System in the Middle Ages*.
== Overview == In postwar studies of medieval {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|ja|日本仏教史|Buddhism in Japan=Buddhism in Japan|lt=Buddhist history of Japan}}, the dominant view was that the old Buddhist schools, the {{ill|Nanto Rokushū|ja|南都六宗|Nanto Rokushū=Nanto Rokushū|lt=Six Nanto Schools}} and the {{ill|Two Heian Schools|ja|平安二宗}} ({{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} and {{ill|Shingon|ja|真言宗}}), existed in a '''mutually exclusive or oppositional''' relationship.
In contrast, Kuroda introduced the term '''Kenmitsu (顕密)'''—which at the time referred to all of Buddhism—as a conceptual framework and proposed the '''Kenmitsu System Theory (顕密体制論)'''.
According to Kuroda, the '''orthodox religion''' in medieval Japan was '''Kenmitsu Buddhism''', which had been integrated '''around {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}''' (密教) since the '''[[Heian period]]'''. The Eight Traditional Buddhist Schools were '''not separate entities''' but functioned within '''a competitive yet unified order''', based on '''a common foundation of esoteric rituals and mystical faiths focused on pacification and incantations'''. Within this system, Esoteric Buddhism (密教) was considered the '''absolute and universal truth'''. Temples of the '''old Buddhist schools''', such as {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}}, maintained '''close ties to state power''', solidifying their position as '''the legitimate religious institutions''' throughout the medieval period. This state of affairs is what Kuroda termed the '''Kenmitsu System'''.
This theory challenged the '''postwar perception''' that saw the {{ill|Kamakura New Buddhism|ja|鎌倉新仏教}} '''(鎌倉新仏教)''' as the '''main representative of medieval Buddhism''', instead emphasizing that '''the old Buddhist schools were the true mainstream of medieval Japanese Buddhism'''. Additionally, by incorporating '''Buddhism into the broader framework of political and social history''', Kuroda's theory—built upon his earlier '''{{ill|Kenmon System Theory|ja|権門体制論}} (権門体制論)'''—introduced '''a new perspective on Buddhist history'''.

Additionally, by incorporating '''Buddhism into the broader framework of political and social history''', Kuroda's theory—built upon his earlier '''{{ill|Kenmon System Theory|ja|権門体制論}} (権門体制論)'''—introduced '''a new perspective on Buddhist history'''.

'''Kenmitsu System''' (顕密体制, Kenmitsu Taisei) is a theory proposed by historian {{ill|Toshio Kuroda|ja|黒田俊雄}} regarding the religious system of medieval {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. It was introduced in the book *The Medieval Japanese State and Religion* (『日本中世の国家と宗教』), published by {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}} in [[1975]], under the title *The Development of the Kenmitsu System in the Middle Ages*.
== Overview == In postwar studies of medieval {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|ja|日本仏教史|Buddhism in Japan=Buddhism in Japan|lt=Buddhist history of Japan}}, the dominant view was that the old Buddhist schools, the {{ill|Nanto Rokushū|ja|南都六宗|Nanto Rokushū=Nanto Rokushū|lt=Six Nanto Schools}} and the {{ill|Two Heian Schools|ja|平安二宗}} ({{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} and {{ill|Shingon|ja|真言宗}}), existed in a '''mutually exclusive or oppositional''' relationship.
In contrast, Kuroda introduced the term '''Kenmitsu (顕密)'''—which at the time referred to all of Buddhism—as a conceptual framework and proposed the '''Kenmitsu System Theory (顕密体制論)'''.
According to Kuroda, the '''orthodox religion''' in medieval Japan was '''Kenmitsu Buddhism''', which had been integrated '''around {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}''' (密教) since the '''[[Heian period]]'''. The Eight Traditional Buddhist Schools were '''not separate entities''' but functioned within '''a competitive yet unified order''', based on '''a common foundation of esoteric rituals and mystical faiths focused on pacification and incantations'''. Within this system, Esoteric Buddhism (密教) was considered the '''absolute and universal truth'''. Temples of the '''old Buddhist schools''', such as {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}}, maintained '''close ties to state power''', solidifying their position as '''the legitimate religious institutions''' throughout the medieval period. This state of affairs is what Kuroda termed the '''Kenmitsu System'''.
This theory challenged the '''postwar perception''' that saw the {{ill|Kamakura New Buddhism|ja|鎌倉新仏教}} '''(鎌倉新仏教)''' as the '''main representative of medieval Buddhism''', instead emphasizing that '''the old Buddhist schools were the true mainstream of medieval Japanese Buddhism'''. Additionally, by incorporating '''Buddhism into the broader framework of political and social history''', Kuroda's theory—built upon his earlier '''{{ill|Kenmon System Theory|ja|権門体制論}} (権門体制論)'''—introduced '''a new perspective on Buddhist history'''.

'''Kenmitsu System''' (顕密体制, Kenmitsu Taisei) is a theory proposed by historian {{ill|Toshio Kuroda|ja|黒田俊雄}} regarding the religious system of medieval {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. It was introduced in the book *The Medieval Japanese State and Religion* (『日本中世の国家と宗教』), published by {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}} in [[1975]], under the title *The Development of the Kenmitsu System in the Middle Ages*.
== Overview == In postwar studies of medieval {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|ja|日本仏教史|Buddhism in Japan=Buddhism in Japan|lt=Buddhist history of Japan}}, the dominant view was that the old Buddhist schools, the {{ill|Nanto Rokushū|ja|南都六宗|Nanto Rokushū=Nanto Rokushū|lt=Six Nanto Schools}} and the {{ill|Two Heian Schools|ja|平安二宗}} ({{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} and {{ill|Shingon|ja|真言宗}}), existed in a '''mutually exclusive or oppositional''' relationship.
In contrast, Kuroda introduced the term '''Kenmitsu (顕密)'''—which at the time referred to all of Buddhism—as a conceptual framework and proposed the '''Kenmitsu System Theory (顕密体制論)'''.
According to Kuroda, the '''orthodox religion''' in medieval Japan was '''Kenmitsu Buddhism''', which had been integrated '''around {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}''' (密教) since the '''[[Heian period]]'''. The Eight Traditional Buddhist Schools were '''not separate entities''' but functioned within '''a competitive yet unified order''', based on '''a common foundation of esoteric rituals and mystical faiths focused on pacification and incantations'''. Within this system, Esoteric Buddhism (密教) was considered the '''absolute and universal truth'''. Temples of the '''old Buddhist schools''', such as {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}}, maintained '''close ties to state power''', solidifying their position as '''the legitimate religious institutions''' throughout the medieval period. This state of affairs is what Kuroda termed the '''Kenmitsu System'''.
This theory challenged the '''postwar perception''' that saw the {{ill|Kamakura New Buddhism|ja|鎌倉新仏教}} '''(鎌倉新仏教)''' as the '''main representative of medieval Buddhism''', instead emphasizing that '''the old Buddhist schools were the true mainstream of medieval Japanese Buddhism'''. Additionally, by incorporating '''Buddhism into the broader framework of political and social history''', Kuroda's theory—built upon his earlier '''{{ill|Kenmon System Theory|ja|権門体制論}} (権門体制論)'''—introduced '''a new perspective on Buddhist history'''.
== See Also ==

'''Kenmitsu System''' (顕密体制, Kenmitsu Taisei) is a theory proposed by historian {{ill|Toshio Kuroda|ja|黒田俊雄}} regarding the religious system of medieval {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. It was introduced in the book *The Medieval Japanese State and Religion* (『日本中世の国家と宗教』), published by {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}} in [[1975]], under the title *The Development of the Kenmitsu System in the Middle Ages*.
== Overview == In postwar studies of medieval {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|ja|日本仏教史|Buddhism in Japan=Buddhism in Japan|lt=Buddhist history of Japan}}, the dominant view was that the old Buddhist schools, the {{ill|Nanto Rokushū|ja|南都六宗|Nanto Rokushū=Nanto Rokushū|lt=Six Nanto Schools}} and the {{ill|Two Heian Schools|ja|平安二宗}} ({{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} and {{ill|Shingon|ja|真言宗}}), existed in a '''mutually exclusive or oppositional''' relationship.
In contrast, Kuroda introduced the term '''Kenmitsu (顕密)'''—which at the time referred to all of Buddhism—as a conceptual framework and proposed the '''Kenmitsu System Theory (顕密体制論)'''.
According to Kuroda, the '''orthodox religion''' in medieval Japan was '''Kenmitsu Buddhism''', which had been integrated '''around {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}''' (密教) since the '''[[Heian period]]'''. The Eight Traditional Buddhist Schools were '''not separate entities''' but functioned within '''a competitive yet unified order''', based on '''a common foundation of esoteric rituals and mystical faiths focused on pacification and incantations'''. Within this system, Esoteric Buddhism (密教) was considered the '''absolute and universal truth'''. Temples of the '''old Buddhist schools''', such as {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}}, maintained '''close ties to state power''', solidifying their position as '''the legitimate religious institutions''' throughout the medieval period. This state of affairs is what Kuroda termed the '''Kenmitsu System'''.
This theory challenged the '''postwar perception''' that saw the {{ill|Kamakura New Buddhism|ja|鎌倉新仏教}} '''(鎌倉新仏教)''' as the '''main representative of medieval Buddhism''', instead emphasizing that '''the old Buddhist schools were the true mainstream of medieval Japanese Buddhism'''. Additionally, by incorporating '''Buddhism into the broader framework of political and social history''', Kuroda's theory—built upon his earlier '''{{ill|Kenmon System Theory|ja|権門体制論}} (権門体制論)'''—introduced '''a new perspective on Buddhist history'''.
== See Also ==

'''Kenmitsu System''' (顕密体制, Kenmitsu Taisei) is a theory proposed by historian {{ill|Toshio Kuroda|ja|黒田俊雄}} regarding the religious system of medieval {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. It was introduced in the book *The Medieval Japanese State and Religion* (『日本中世の国家と宗教』), published by {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}} in [[1975]], under the title *The Development of the Kenmitsu System in the Middle Ages*.
== Overview == In postwar studies of medieval {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|ja|日本仏教史|Buddhism in Japan=Buddhism in Japan|lt=Buddhist history of Japan}}, the dominant view was that the old Buddhist schools, the {{ill|Nanto Rokushū|ja|南都六宗|Nanto Rokushū=Nanto Rokushū|lt=Six Nanto Schools}} and the {{ill|Two Heian Schools|ja|平安二宗}} ({{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} and {{ill|Shingon|ja|真言宗}}), existed in a '''mutually exclusive or oppositional''' relationship.
In contrast, Kuroda introduced the term '''Kenmitsu (顕密)'''—which at the time referred to all of Buddhism—as a conceptual framework and proposed the '''Kenmitsu System Theory (顕密体制論)'''.
According to Kuroda, the '''orthodox religion''' in medieval Japan was '''Kenmitsu Buddhism''', which had been integrated '''around {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}''' (密教) since the '''[[Heian period]]'''. The Eight Traditional Buddhist Schools were '''not separate entities''' but functioned within '''a competitive yet unified order''', based on '''a common foundation of esoteric rituals and mystical faiths focused on pacification and incantations'''. Within this system, Esoteric Buddhism (密教) was considered the '''absolute and universal truth'''. Temples of the '''old Buddhist schools''', such as {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}}, maintained '''close ties to state power''', solidifying their position as '''the legitimate religious institutions''' throughout the medieval period. This state of affairs is what Kuroda termed the '''Kenmitsu System'''.
This theory challenged the '''postwar perception''' that saw the {{ill|Kamakura New Buddhism|ja|鎌倉新仏教}} '''(鎌倉新仏教)''' as the '''main representative of medieval Buddhism''', instead emphasizing that '''the old Buddhist schools were the true mainstream of medieval Japanese Buddhism'''. Additionally, by incorporating '''Buddhism into the broader framework of political and social history''', Kuroda's theory—built upon his earlier '''{{ill|Kenmon System Theory|ja|権門体制論}} (権門体制論)'''—introduced '''a new perspective on Buddhist history'''.

revision.text:
'''Kenmitsu System''' (顕密体制, Kenmitsu Taisei) is a theory proposed by historian {{ill|Toshio Kuroda|ja|黒田俊雄}} regarding the religious system of medieval {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. It was introduced in the book *The Medieval Japanese State and Religion* (『日本中世の国家と宗教』), published by {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}} in [[1975]], under the title *The Development of the Kenmitsu System in the Middle Ages*.
== Overview == In postwar studies of medieval {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|ja|日本仏教史|Buddhism in Japan=Buddhism in Japan|lt=Buddhist history of Japan}}, the dominant view was that the old Buddhist schools, the {{ill|Nanto Rokushū|ja|南都六宗|Nanto Rokushū=Nanto Rokushū|lt=Six Nanto Schools}} and the {{ill|Two Heian Schools|ja|平安二宗}} ({{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} and {{ill|Shingon|ja|真言宗}}), existed in a '''mutually exclusive or oppositional''' relationship.
In contrast, Kuroda introduced the term '''Kenmitsu (顕密)'''—which at the time referred to all of Buddhism—as a conceptual framework and proposed the '''Kenmitsu System Theory (顕密体制論)'''.
According to Kuroda, the '''orthodox religion''' in medieval Japan was '''Kenmitsu Buddhism''', which had been integrated '''around {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}''' (密教) since the '''[[Heian period]]'''. The Eight Traditional Buddhist Schools were '''not separate entities''' but functioned within '''a competitive yet unified order''', based on '''a common foundation of esoteric rituals and mystical faiths focused on pacification and incantations'''. Within this system, Esoteric Buddhism (密教) was considered the '''absolute and universal truth'''. Temples of the '''old Buddhist schools''', such as {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}}, maintained '''close ties to state power''', solidifying their position as '''the legitimate religious institutions''' throughout the medieval period. This state of affairs is what Kuroda termed the '''Kenmitsu System'''.
This theory challenged the '''postwar perception''' that saw the {{ill|Kamakura New Buddhism|ja|鎌倉新仏教}} '''(鎌倉新仏教)''' as the '''main representative of medieval Buddhism''', instead emphasizing that '''the old Buddhist schools were the true mainstream of medieval Japanese Buddhism'''. Additionally, by incorporating '''Buddhism into the broader framework of political and social history''', Kuroda's theory—built upon his earlier '''{{ill|Kenmon System Theory|ja|権門体制論}} (権門体制論)'''—introduced '''a new perspective on Buddhist history'''.
== See Also ==

The Eight Traditional Buddhist Schools were '''not separate entities''' but functioned within '''a competitive yet unified order''', based on '''a common foundation of esoteric rituals and mystical faiths focused on pacification and incantations'''. '''Kenmitsu System''' (顕密体制, Kenmitsu Taisei) is a theory proposed by historian {{ill|Toshio Kuroda|ja|黒田俊雄}} regarding the religious system of medieval {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. It was introduced in the book *The Medieval Japanese State and Religion* (『日本中世の国家と宗教』), published by {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}} in [[1975]], under the title *The Development of the Kenmitsu System in the Middle Ages*.
== Overview == In postwar studies of medieval {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|ja|日本仏教史|Buddhism in Japan=Buddhism in Japan|lt=Buddhist history of Japan}}, the dominant view was that the old Buddhist schools, the {{ill|Nanto Rokushū|ja|南都六宗|Nanto Rokushū=Nanto Rokushū|lt=Six Nanto Schools}} and the {{ill|Two Heian Schools|ja|平安二宗}} ({{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} and {{ill|Shingon|ja|真言宗}}), existed in a '''mutually exclusive or oppositional''' relationship.
In contrast, Kuroda introduced the term '''Kenmitsu (顕密)'''—which at the time referred to all of Buddhism—as a conceptual framework and proposed the '''Kenmitsu System Theory (顕密体制論)'''.
According to Kuroda, the '''orthodox religion''' in medieval Japan was '''Kenmitsu Buddhism''', which had been integrated '''around {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}''' (密教) since the '''[[Heian period]]'''. The Eight Traditional Buddhist Schools were '''not separate entities''' but functioned within '''a competitive yet unified order''', based on '''a common foundation of esoteric rituals and mystical faiths focused on pacification and incantations'''. Within this system, Esoteric Buddhism (密教) was considered the '''absolute and universal truth'''. Temples of the '''old Buddhist schools''', such as {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}}, maintained '''close ties to state power''', solidifying their position as '''the legitimate religious institutions''' throughout the medieval period. This state of affairs is what Kuroda termed the '''Kenmitsu System'''.
This theory challenged the '''postwar perception''' that saw the {{ill|Kamakura New Buddhism|ja|鎌倉新仏教}} '''(鎌倉新仏教)''' as the '''main representative of medieval Buddhism''', instead emphasizing that '''the old Buddhist schools were the true mainstream of medieval Japanese Buddhism'''. Additionally, by incorporating '''Buddhism into the broader framework of political and social history''', Kuroda's theory—built upon his earlier '''{{ill|Kenmon System Theory|ja|権門体制論}} (権門体制論)'''—introduced '''a new perspective on Buddhist history'''.
== See Also ==

revision.text:
'''顕密体制'''（けんみつたいせい）とは、歴史学者[[黒田俊雄]]が提唱した[[日本]]の[[中世]][[宗教]]体制に関する[[学説]]。[[岩波書店]]『日本中世の国家と宗教』（[[1975年]]）で「中世における顕密体制の展開」というタイトルで発表した。
== 概略 == 戦後の中世[[仏教史]]では、旧仏教である[[南都六宗]]と[[平安二宗]]（[[天台宗]]、[[真言宗]]）は対等な存在として対立的または排他的な関係のもとに併立しているとの見方が大勢を占めていた。
それに対し黒田は、文献に現れる[[顕密]]（当時は一切の仏教を表現する用語）という言葉を用語化して顕密体制論という学説を提唱した。
実際に日本中世において正統と見なされた宗教は、[[平安時代]]以来[[密教]]を基軸に統合された顕密の仏教であり、旧仏教八宗は併立していたのではなく、[[密教]]に見ることが出来る鎮魂呪術的信仰という共通の基盤の上に密教の絶対的・普遍的真実性を前提とした競合的な秩序を形成していたものであるとし、[[延暦寺]]などの旧仏教系寺院が、このような秩序を保ちつつ[[国家]][[権力]]と密着し、[[正統]]な宗教としてのあり方を固めた体制が中世を通じて見られたとするのが顕密体制論である。
この理論により、戦後見られた[[鎌倉新仏教]]を中世仏教の代表と見なす見解に対し、旧仏教が中世仏教の本流であるという認識が生まれた。また、黒田の提唱した[[権門体制論]]の国家像を前提としながら、政治社会史全体の構図の中に仏教を取り入れることにより、仏教史に新たな視点を追加した。
== 関連項目 == *[[寺社勢力]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:けんみつたいせい}} [[Category:日本の制度史]] [[Category:日本の仏教史]] [[Category:鎌倉時代の仏教]] [[Category:日本の史学史]] {{Japanese-history-stub}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:顕密体制]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11665110]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11665110]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kenmitsu System''' (顕密体制, Kenmitsu Taisei) is a theory proposed by historian [[:en:Toshio Kuroda (Shinto professor)|Toshio Kuroda]] regarding the religious system of medieval [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It was introduced in the book *The Medieval Japanese State and Religion* (『日本中世の国家と宗教』), published by [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]] in [[:en:1975|1975]], under the title *The Development of the Kenmitsu System in the Middle Ages*.
== Overview == In postwar studies of medieval {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|ja|日本仏教史|Buddhism in Japan=Buddhism in Japan|lt=Buddhist history of Japan|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhism in Japan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の仏教|1=Buddhism in Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q736311}}, the dominant view was that the old Buddhist schools, the [[:en:Nanto Rokushū|Six Nanto Schools]] and the {{ill|Two Heian Schools|ja|平安二宗|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Two Heian Schools|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平安仏教|1=Heian Buddhism|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11482387}} ([[:en:Tiantai|Tendai]] and [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon]]), existed in a '''mutually exclusive or oppositional''' relationship.
In contrast, Kuroda introduced the term '''Kenmitsu (顕密)'''—which at the time referred to all of Buddhism—as a conceptual framework and proposed the '''Kenmitsu System Theory (顕密体制論)'''.
According to Kuroda, the '''orthodox religion''' in medieval Japan was '''Kenmitsu Buddhism''', which had been integrated '''around [[:en:Mikkyō|Esoteric Buddhism]]''' (密教) since the '''[[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]'''. The Eight Traditional Buddhist Schools were '''not separate entities''' but functioned within '''a competitive yet unified order''', based on '''a common foundation of esoteric rituals and mystical faiths focused on pacification and incantations'''. Within this system, Esoteric Buddhism (密教) was considered the '''absolute and universal truth'''. Temples of the '''old Buddhist schools''', such as [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]], maintained '''close ties to state power''', solidifying their position as '''the legitimate religious institutions''' throughout the medieval period. This state of affairs is what Kuroda termed the '''Kenmitsu System'''.
This theory challenged the '''postwar perception''' that saw the {{ill|Kamakura Buddhism|lt=Kamakura Buddhism|lt=Kamakura New Buddhism|ja|鎌倉新仏教|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura New Buddhism|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鎌倉仏教}} '''(鎌倉新仏教)''' as the '''main representative of medieval Buddhism''', instead emphasizing that '''the old Buddhist schools were the true mainstream of medieval Japanese Buddhism'''. Additionally, by incorporating '''Buddhism into the broader framework of political and social history''', Kuroda's theory—built upon his earlier '''{{ill|Kenmon System Theory|ja|権門体制論|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmon System Theory|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 権門体制}} (権門体制論)'''—introduced '''a new perspective on Buddhist history'''.
== See Also ==

{{Japanese-history-stub}}

* [[:en:Right of asylum|Right of asylum]]
* {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple Precinct Cities|qq=}}

* {{ill|Sōhei|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōhei|qq=}}

It was introduced in the book *The Medieval Japanese State and Religion* (『日本中世の国家と宗教』), published by [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]] in [[:en:1975|1975]], under the title *The Development of the Kenmitsu System in the Middle Ages*.
== Overview == In postwar studies of medieval {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|ja|日本仏教史|Buddhism in Japan=Buddhism in Japan|lt=Buddhist history of Japan|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhism in Japan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の仏教|1=Buddhism in Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q736311}}, the dominant view was that the old Buddhist schools, the [[:en:Nanto Rokushū|Six Nanto Schools]] and the {{ill|Two Heian Schools|ja|平安二宗|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Two Heian Schools|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平安仏教|1=Heian Buddhism|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11482387}} ([[:en:Tiantai|Tendai]] and [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon]]), existed in a '''mutually exclusive or oppositional''' relationship.
In contrast, Kuroda introduced the term '''Kenmitsu (顕密)'''—which at the time referred to all of Buddhism—as a conceptual framework and proposed the '''Kenmitsu System Theory (顕密体制論)'''.
According to Kuroda, the '''orthodox religion''' in medieval Japan was '''Kenmitsu Buddhism''', which had been integrated '''around [[:en:Mikkyō|Esoteric Buddhism]]''' (密教) since the '''[[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]'''. The Eight Traditional Buddhist Schools were '''not separate entities''' but functioned within '''a competitive yet unified order''', based on '''a common foundation of esoteric rituals and mystical faiths focused on pacification and incantations'''. Within this system, Esoteric Buddhism (密教) was considered the '''absolute and universal truth'''. Temples of the '''old Buddhist schools''', such as [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]], maintained '''close ties to state power''', solidifying their position as '''the legitimate religious institutions''' throughout the medieval period. This state of affairs is what Kuroda termed the '''Kenmitsu System'''.
This theory challenged the '''postwar perception''' that saw the {{ill|Kamakura Buddhism|lt=Kamakura Buddhism|lt=Kamakura New Buddhism|ja|鎌倉新仏教|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura New Buddhism|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鎌倉仏教}} '''(鎌倉新仏教)''' as the '''main representative of medieval Buddhism''', instead emphasizing that '''the old Buddhist schools were the true mainstream of medieval Japanese Buddhism'''. Additionally, by incorporating '''Buddhism into the broader framework of political and social history''', Kuroda's theory—built upon his earlier '''{{ill|Kenmon System Theory|ja|権門体制論|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmon System Theory|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 権門体制}} (権門体制論)'''—introduced '''a new perspective on Buddhist history'''.

This theory challenged the '''postwar perception''' that saw the {{ill|Kamakura Buddhism|lt=Kamakura Buddhism|lt=Kamakura New Buddhism|ja|鎌倉新仏教|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura New Buddhism|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鎌倉仏教}} '''(鎌倉新仏教)''' as the '''main representative of medieval Buddhism''', instead emphasizing that '''the old Buddhist schools were the true mainstream of medieval Japanese Buddhism'''. Additionally, by incorporating '''Buddhism into the broader framework of political and social history''', Kuroda's theory—built upon his earlier '''{{ill|Kenmon System Theory|ja|権門体制論|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmon System Theory|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 権門体制}} (権門体制論)'''—introduced '''a new perspective on Buddhist history'''.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:顕密体制]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11665110]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11665110]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kenmitsu System''' (顕密体制, Kenmitsu Taisei) is a theory proposed by historian [[:en:Toshio Kuroda (Shinto professor)|Toshio Kuroda]] regarding the religious system of medieval [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It was introduced in the book *The Medieval Japanese State and Religion* (『日本中世の国家と宗教』), published by [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]] in [[:en:1975|1975]], under the title *The Development of the Kenmitsu System in the Middle Ages*.
== Overview == In postwar studies of medieval {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|ja|日本仏教史|Buddhism in Japan=Buddhism in Japan|lt=Buddhist history of Japan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhism in Japan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の仏教|1=Buddhism in Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q736311}}, the dominant view was that the old Buddhist schools, the [[:en:Nanto Rokushū|Six Nanto Schools]] and the {{ill|Two Heian Schools|ja|平安二宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Two Heian Schools|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平安仏教|1=Heian Buddhism|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11482387}} ([[:en:Tiantai|Tendai]] and [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon]]), existed in a '''mutually exclusive or oppositional''' relationship.
In contrast, Kuroda introduced the term '''Kenmitsu (顕密)'''—which at the time referred to all of Buddhism—as a conceptual framework and proposed the '''Kenmitsu System Theory (顕密体制論)'''.
According to Kuroda, the '''orthodox religion''' in medieval Japan was '''Kenmitsu Buddhism''', which had been integrated '''around [[:en:Mikkyō|Esoteric Buddhism]]''' (密教) since the '''[[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]'''. The Eight Traditional Buddhist Schools were '''not separate entities''' but functioned within '''a competitive yet unified order''', based on '''a common foundation of esoteric rituals and mystical faiths focused on pacification and incantations'''. Within this system, Esoteric Buddhism (密教) was considered the '''absolute and universal truth'''. Temples of the '''old Buddhist schools''', such as [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]], maintained '''close ties to state power''', solidifying their position as '''the legitimate religious institutions''' throughout the medieval period. This state of affairs is what Kuroda termed the '''Kenmitsu System'''.
This theory challenged the '''postwar perception''' that saw the {{ill|Kamakura Buddhism|lt=Kamakura Buddhism|lt=Kamakura New Buddhism|ja|鎌倉新仏教|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura New Buddhism|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鎌倉仏教}} '''(鎌倉新仏教)''' as the '''main representative of medieval Buddhism''', instead emphasizing that '''the old Buddhist schools were the true mainstream of medieval Japanese Buddhism'''. Additionally, by incorporating '''Buddhism into the broader framework of political and social history''', Kuroda's theory—built upon his earlier '''{{ill|Kenmon System Theory|ja|権門体制論|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmon System Theory|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 権門体制}} (権門体制論)'''—introduced '''a new perspective on Buddhist history'''.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:顕密体制]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11665110]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11665110]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kenmitsu System''' (顕密体制, Kenmitsu Taisei) is a theory proposed by historian [[:en:Toshio Kuroda (Shinto professor)|Toshio Kuroda]] regarding the religious system of medieval [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It was introduced in the book *The Medieval Japanese State and Religion* (『日本中世の国家と宗教』), published by [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]] in [[:en:1975|1975]], under the title *The Development of the Kenmitsu System in the Middle Ages*.
== Overview == In postwar studies of medieval {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|ja|日本仏教史|Buddhism in Japan=Buddhism in Japan|lt=Buddhist history of Japan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhism in Japan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の仏教|1=Buddhism in Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q736311}}, the dominant view was that the old Buddhist schools, the [[:en:Nanto Rokushū|Six Nanto Schools]] and the {{ill|Two Heian Schools|ja|平安二宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Two Heian Schools|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平安仏教|1=Heian Buddhism|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11482387}} ([[:en:Tiantai|Tendai]] and [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon]]), existed in a '''mutually exclusive or oppositional''' relationship.
In contrast, Kuroda introduced the term '''Kenmitsu (顕密)'''—which at the time referred to all of Buddhism—as a conceptual framework and proposed the '''Kenmitsu System Theory (顕密体制論)'''.
According to Kuroda, the '''orthodox religion''' in medieval Japan was '''Kenmitsu Buddhism''', which had been integrated '''around [[:en:Mikkyō|Esoteric Buddhism]]''' (密教) since the '''[[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]'''. The Eight Traditional Buddhist Schools were '''not separate entities''' but functioned within '''a competitive yet unified order''', based on '''a common foundation of esoteric rituals and mystical faiths focused on pacification and incantations'''. Within this system, Esoteric Buddhism (密教) was considered the '''absolute and universal truth'''. Temples of the '''old Buddhist schools''', such as [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]], maintained '''close ties to state power''', solidifying their position as '''the legitimate religious institutions''' throughout the medieval period. This state of affairs is what Kuroda termed the '''Kenmitsu System'''.
This theory challenged the '''postwar perception''' that saw the {{ill|Kamakura Buddhism|lt=Kamakura Buddhism|lt=Kamakura New Buddhism|ja|鎌倉新仏教|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura New Buddhism|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鎌倉仏教}} '''(鎌倉新仏教)''' as the '''main representative of medieval Buddhism''', instead emphasizing that '''the old Buddhist schools were the true mainstream of medieval Japanese Buddhism'''. Additionally, by incorporating '''Buddhism into the broader framework of political and social history''', Kuroda's theory—built upon his earlier '''{{ill|Kenmon System Theory|ja|権門体制論|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmon System Theory|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 権門体制}} (権門体制論)'''—introduced '''a new perspective on Buddhist history'''.
== See Also ==

In contrast, Kuroda introduced the term '''Kenmitsu (顕密)'''—which at the time referred to all of Buddhism—as a conceptual framework and proposed the '''Kenmitsu System Theory (顕密体制論)'''.
According to Kuroda, the '''orthodox religion''' in medieval Japan was '''Kenmitsu Buddhism''', which had been integrated '''around [[:en:Mikkyō|Esoteric Buddhism]]''' (密教) since the '''[[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]'''. The Eight Traditional Buddhist Schools were '''not separate entities''' but functioned within '''a competitive yet unified order''', based on '''a common foundation of esoteric rituals and mystical faiths focused on pacification and incantations'''. Within this system, Esoteric Buddhism (密教) was considered the '''absolute and universal truth'''. Temples of the '''old Buddhist schools''', such as [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]], maintained '''close ties to state power''', solidifying their position as '''the legitimate religious institutions''' throughout the medieval period. This state of affairs is what Kuroda termed the '''Kenmitsu System'''.
This theory challenged the '''postwar perception''' that saw the {{ill|Kamakura Buddhism|lt=Kamakura Buddhism|lt=Kamakura New Buddhism|ja|鎌倉新仏教|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura New Buddhism|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鎌倉仏教}} '''(鎌倉新仏教)''' as the '''main representative of medieval Buddhism''', instead emphasizing that '''the old Buddhist schools were the true mainstream of medieval Japanese Buddhism'''. Additionally, by incorporating '''Buddhism into the broader framework of political and social history''', Kuroda's theory—built upon his earlier '''{{ill|Kenmon System Theory|ja|権門体制論|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmon System Theory|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 権門体制}} (権門体制論)'''—introduced '''a new perspective on Buddhist history'''.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:顕密体制]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:顕密体制]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:顕密体制]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11665110]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11665110]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kenmitsu System''' (顕密体制, Kenmitsu Taisei) is a theory proposed by historian [[:en:Toshio Kuroda (Shinto professor)|Toshio Kuroda]] regarding the religious system of medieval [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It was introduced in the book *The Medieval Japanese State and Religion* (『日本中世の国家と宗教』), published by [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]] in [[:en:1975|1975]], under the title *The Development of the Kenmitsu System in the Middle Ages*.
== Overview == In postwar studies of medieval {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|ja|日本仏教史|Buddhism in Japan=Buddhism in Japan|lt=Buddhist history of Japan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhism in Japan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の仏教|1=Buddhism in Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q736311}}, the dominant view was that the old Buddhist schools, the [[:en:Nanto Rokushū|Six Nanto Schools]] and the {{ill|Two Heian Schools|ja|平安二宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Two Heian Schools|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平安仏教|1=Heian Buddhism|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11482387}} ([[:en:Tiantai|Tendai]] and [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon]]), existed in a '''mutually exclusive or oppositional''' relationship.
In contrast, Kuroda introduced the term '''Kenmitsu (顕密)'''—which at the time referred to all of Buddhism—as a conceptual framework and proposed the '''Kenmitsu System Theory (顕密体制論)'''.
According to Kuroda, the '''orthodox religion''' in medieval Japan was '''Kenmitsu Buddhism''', which had been integrated '''around [[:en:Mikkyō|Esoteric Buddhism]]''' (密教) since the '''[[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]'''. The Eight Traditional Buddhist Schools were '''not separate entities''' but functioned within '''a competitive yet unified order''', based on '''a common foundation of esoteric rituals and mystical faiths focused on pacification and incantations'''. Within this system, Esoteric Buddhism (密教) was considered the '''absolute and universal truth'''. Temples of the '''old Buddhist schools''', such as [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]], maintained '''close ties to state power''', solidifying their position as '''the legitimate religious institutions''' throughout the medieval period. This state of affairs is what Kuroda termed the '''Kenmitsu System'''.
This theory challenged the '''postwar perception''' that saw the {{ill|Kamakura Buddhism|lt=Kamakura Buddhism|lt=Kamakura New Buddhism|ja|鎌倉新仏教|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura New Buddhism|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鎌倉仏教}} '''(鎌倉新仏教)''' as the '''main representative of medieval Buddhism''', instead emphasizing that '''the old Buddhist schools were the true mainstream of medieval Japanese Buddhism'''. Additionally, by incorporating '''Buddhism into the broader framework of political and social history''', Kuroda's theory—built upon his earlier '''{{ill|Kenmon System Theory|ja|権門体制論|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmon System Theory|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 権門体制}} (権門体制論)'''—introduced '''a new perspective on Buddhist history'''.
== See Also ==

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:顕密体制]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:顕密体制]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:顕密体制]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11665110]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11665110]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kenmitsu System''' (顕密体制, Kenmitsu Taisei) is a theory proposed by historian {{ill|Toshio Kuroda (Shinto professor)|en|Toshio Kuroda (Shinto professor)|WD=Q7827765|lt=Toshio Kuroda}} regarding the religious system of medieval {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. It was introduced in the book *The Medieval Japanese State and Religion* (『日本中世の国家と宗教』), published by {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|en|Iwanami Shoten|WD=Q1331427|lt=Iwanami Shoten}} in {{ill|1975|en|1975|WD=Q2479|lt=1975}}, under the title *The Development of the Kenmitsu System in the Middle Ages*.
== Overview == In postwar studies of medieval {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|ja|日本仏教史|Buddhism in Japan=Buddhism in Japan|lt=Buddhist history of Japan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhism in Japan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の仏教|1=Buddhism in Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q736311}}, the dominant view was that the old Buddhist schools, the {{ill|Nanto Rokushū|en|Nanto Rokushū|WD=Q5366159|lt=Six Nanto Schools}} and the {{ill|Two Heian Schools|ja|平安二宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Two Heian Schools|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平安仏教|1=Heian Buddhism|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11482387}} ({{ill|Tiantai|en|Tiantai|WD=Q668188|lt=Tendai}} and {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|WD=Q550182|lt=Shingon}}), existed in a '''mutually exclusive or oppositional''' relationship.
In contrast, Kuroda introduced the term '''Kenmitsu (顕密)'''—which at the time referred to all of Buddhism—as a conceptual framework and proposed the '''Kenmitsu System Theory (顕密体制論)'''.
According to Kuroda, the '''orthodox religion''' in medieval Japan was '''Kenmitsu Buddhism''', which had been integrated '''around {{ill|Mikkyō|en|Mikkyō|WD=Q336102|lt=Esoteric Buddhism}}''' (密教) since the '''{{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}'''. The Eight Traditional Buddhist Schools were '''not separate entities''' but functioned within '''a competitive yet unified order''', based on '''a common foundation of esoteric rituals and mystical faiths focused on pacification and incantations'''. Within this system, Esoteric Buddhism (密教) was considered the '''absolute and universal truth'''. Temples of the '''old Buddhist schools''', such as {{ill|Enryaku-ji|en|Enryaku-ji|WD=Q917195|lt=Enryaku-ji}}, maintained '''close ties to state power''', solidifying their position as '''the legitimate religious institutions''' throughout the medieval period. This state of affairs is what Kuroda termed the '''Kenmitsu System'''.
This theory challenged the '''postwar perception''' that saw the {{ill|Kamakura Buddhism|lt=Kamakura Buddhism|lt=Kamakura New Buddhism|ja|鎌倉新仏教|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura New Buddhism|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鎌倉仏教}} '''(鎌倉新仏教)''' as the '''main representative of medieval Buddhism''', instead emphasizing that '''the old Buddhist schools were the true mainstream of medieval Japanese Buddhism'''. Additionally, by incorporating '''Buddhism into the broader framework of political and social history''', Kuroda's theory—built upon his earlier '''{{ill|Kenmon System Theory|ja|権門体制論|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmon System Theory|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 権門体制}} (権門体制論)'''—introduced '''a new perspective on Buddhist history'''.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:顕密体制]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:顕密体制]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:顕密体制]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11665110]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11665110]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kenmitsu System''' (顕密体制, Kenmitsu Taisei) is a theory proposed by historian {{ill|Toshio Kuroda (Shinto professor)|en|Toshio Kuroda (Shinto professor)|WD=Q7827765|lt=Toshio Kuroda}} regarding the religious system of medieval {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. It was introduced in the book *The Medieval Japanese State and Religion* (『日本中世の国家と宗教』), published by {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|en|Iwanami Shoten|WD=Q1331427|lt=Iwanami Shoten}} in {{ill|1975|en|1975|WD=Q2479|lt=1975}}, under the title *The Development of the Kenmitsu System in the Middle Ages*.
== Overview == In postwar studies of medieval {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|ja|日本仏教史|Buddhism in Japan=Buddhism in Japan|lt=Buddhist history of Japan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhism in Japan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の仏教|1=Buddhism in Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q736311}}, the dominant view was that the old Buddhist schools, the {{ill|Nanto Rokushū|en|Nanto Rokushū|WD=Q5366159|lt=Six Nanto Schools}} and the {{ill|Two Heian Schools|ja|平安二宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Two Heian Schools|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平安仏教|1=Heian Buddhism|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11482387}} ({{ill|Tiantai|en|Tiantai|WD=Q668188|lt=Tendai}} and {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|WD=Q550182|lt=Shingon}}), existed in a '''mutually exclusive or oppositional''' relationship.
In contrast, Kuroda introduced the term '''Kenmitsu (顕密)'''—which at the time referred to all of Buddhism—as a conceptual framework and proposed the '''Kenmitsu System Theory (顕密体制論)'''.
According to Kuroda, the '''orthodox religion''' in medieval Japan was '''Kenmitsu Buddhism''', which had been integrated '''around {{ill|Mikkyō|en|Mikkyō|WD=Q336102|lt=Esoteric Buddhism}}''' (密教) since the '''{{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}'''. The Eight Traditional Buddhist Schools were '''not separate entities''' but functioned within '''a competitive yet unified order''', based on '''a common foundation of esoteric rituals and mystical faiths focused on pacification and incantations'''. Within this system, Esoteric Buddhism (密教) was considered the '''absolute and universal truth'''. Temples of the '''old Buddhist schools''', such as {{ill|Enryaku-ji|en|Enryaku-ji|WD=Q917195|lt=Enryaku-ji}}, maintained '''close ties to state power''', solidifying their position as '''the legitimate religious institutions''' throughout the medieval period. This state of affairs is what Kuroda termed the '''Kenmitsu System'''.
This theory challenged the '''postwar perception''' that saw the {{ill|Kamakura Buddhism|lt=Kamakura Buddhism|lt=Kamakura New Buddhism|ja|鎌倉新仏教|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura New Buddhism|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鎌倉仏教}} '''(鎌倉新仏教)''' as the '''main representative of medieval Buddhism''', instead emphasizing that '''the old Buddhist schools were the true mainstream of medieval Japanese Buddhism'''. Additionally, by incorporating '''Buddhism into the broader framework of political and social history''', Kuroda's theory—built upon his earlier '''{{ill|Kenmon System Theory|ja|権門体制論|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmon System Theory|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 権門体制}} (権門体制論)'''—introduced '''a new perspective on Buddhist history'''.
== See Also ==

'''Kenmitsu System''' (顕密体制, Kenmitsu Taisei) is a theory proposed by historian {{ill|Toshio Kuroda (Shinto professor)|en|Toshio Kuroda (Shinto professor)|WD=Q7827765|lt=Toshio Kuroda}} regarding the religious system of medieval {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. It was introduced in the book *The Medieval Japanese State and Religion* (『日本中世の国家と宗教』), published by {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|en|Iwanami Shoten|WD=Q1331427|lt=Iwanami Shoten}} in {{ill|1975|en|1975|WD=Q2479|lt=1975}}, under the title *The Development of the Kenmitsu System in the Middle Ages*.
== Overview == In postwar studies of medieval {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|ja|日本仏教史|Buddhism in Japan=Buddhism in Japan|lt=Buddhist history of Japan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhism in Japan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の仏教|1=Buddhism in Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q736311}}, the dominant view was that the old Buddhist schools, the {{ill|Nanto Rokushū|en|Nanto Rokushū|WD=Q5366159|lt=Six Nanto Schools}} and the {{ill|Two Heian Schools|ja|平安二宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Two Heian Schools|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平安仏教|1=Heian Buddhism|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11482387}} ({{ill|Tiantai|en|Tiantai|WD=Q668188|lt=Tendai}} and {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|WD=Q550182|lt=Shingon}}), existed in a '''mutually exclusive or oppositional''' relationship.
In contrast, Kuroda introduced the term '''Kenmitsu (顕密)'''—which at the time referred to all of Buddhism—as a conceptual framework and proposed the '''Kenmitsu System Theory (顕密体制論)'''.
According to Kuroda, the '''orthodox religion''' in medieval Japan was '''Kenmitsu Buddhism''', which had been integrated '''around {{ill|Mikkyō|en|Mikkyō|WD=Q336102|lt=Esoteric Buddhism}}''' (密教) since the '''{{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}'''. The Eight Traditional Buddhist Schools were '''not separate entities''' but functioned within '''a competitive yet unified order''', based on '''a common foundation of esoteric rituals and mystical faiths focused on pacification and incantations'''. Within this system, Esoteric Buddhism (密教) was considered the '''absolute and universal truth'''. Temples of the '''old Buddhist schools''', such as {{ill|Enryaku-ji|en|Enryaku-ji|WD=Q917195|lt=Enryaku-ji}}, maintained '''close ties to state power''', solidifying their position as '''the legitimate religious institutions''' throughout the medieval period. This state of affairs is what Kuroda termed the '''Kenmitsu System'''.
This theory challenged the '''postwar perception''' that saw the {{ill|Kamakura Buddhism|lt=Kamakura Buddhism|lt=Kamakura New Buddhism|ja|鎌倉新仏教|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura New Buddhism|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鎌倉仏教}} '''(鎌倉新仏教)''' as the '''main representative of medieval Buddhism''', instead emphasizing that '''the old Buddhist schools were the true mainstream of medieval Japanese Buddhism'''. Additionally, by incorporating '''Buddhism into the broader framework of political and social history''', Kuroda's theory—built upon his earlier '''{{ill|Kenmon System Theory|ja|権門体制論|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmon System Theory|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 権門体制}} (権門体制論)'''—introduced '''a new perspective on Buddhist history'''.
== See Also ==

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:顕密体制]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:顕密体制]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:顕密体制]]

'''Kenmitsu System''' (顕密体制, Kenmitsu Taisei) is a theory proposed by historian {{ill|Toshio Kuroda (Shinto professor)|en|Toshio Kuroda (Shinto professor)|WD=Q7827765|lt=Toshio Kuroda}} regarding the religious system of medieval {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. According to Kuroda, the '''orthodox religion''' in medieval Japan was '''Kenmitsu Buddhism''', which had been integrated '''around {{ill|Mikkyō|en|Mikkyō|WD=Q336102|lt=Esoteric Buddhism}}''' (密教) since the '''{{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}'''. The Eight Traditional Buddhist Schools were '''not separate entities''' but functioned within '''a competitive yet unified order''', based on '''a common foundation of esoteric rituals and mystical faiths focused on pacification and incantations'''. Within this system, Esoteric Buddhism (密教) was considered the '''absolute and universal truth'''. Temples of the '''old Buddhist schools''', such as {{ill|Enryaku-ji|en|Enryaku-ji|WD=Q917195|lt=Enryaku-ji}}, maintained '''close ties to state power''', solidifying their position as '''the legitimate religious institutions''' throughout the medieval period. This state of affairs is what Kuroda termed the '''Kenmitsu System'''.
This theory challenged the '''postwar perception''' that saw the {{ill|Kamakura Buddhism|lt=Kamakura Buddhism|lt=Kamakura New Buddhism|ja|鎌倉新仏教|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura New Buddhism|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鎌倉仏教}} '''(鎌倉新仏教)''' as the '''main representative of medieval Buddhism''', instead emphasizing that '''the old Buddhist schools were the true mainstream of medieval Japanese Buddhism'''. Additionally, by incorporating '''Buddhism into the broader framework of political and social history''', Kuroda's theory—built upon his earlier '''{{ill|Kenmon System Theory|ja|権門体制論|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmon System Theory|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 権門体制}} (権門体制論)'''—introduced '''a new perspective on Buddhist history'''.

'''Kenmitsu System''' (顕密体制, Kenmitsu Taisei) is a theory proposed by historian {{ill|Toshio Kuroda (Shinto professor)|en|Toshio Kuroda (Shinto professor)|WD=Q7827765|lt=Toshio Kuroda|1=Toshio Kuroda (Shinto professor)}} regarding the religious system of medieval {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. It was introduced in the book *The Medieval Japanese State and Religion* (『日本中世の国家と宗教』), published by {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|en|Iwanami Shoten|WD=Q1331427|lt=Iwanami Shoten|1=Iwanami Shoten}} in {{ill|1975|en|1975|WD=Q2479|lt=1975|1=1975}}, under the title *The Development of the Kenmitsu System in the Middle Ages*.
== Overview == In postwar studies of medieval {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|ja|日本仏教史|Buddhism in Japan=Buddhism in Japan|lt=Buddhist history of Japan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhism in Japan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の仏教|1=Buddhism in Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q736311}}, the dominant view was that the old Buddhist schools, the {{ill|Nanto Rokushū|en|Nanto Rokushū|WD=Q5366159|lt=Six Nanto Schools|1=Nanto Rokushū}} and the {{ill|Two Heian Schools|ja|平安二宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Two Heian Schools|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平安仏教|1=Heian Buddhism|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11482387}} ({{ill|Tiantai|en|Tiantai|WD=Q668188|lt=Tendai|1=Tiantai}} and {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|WD=Q550182|lt=Shingon|1=Shingon Buddhism}}), existed in a '''mutually exclusive or oppositional''' relationship.
In contrast, Kuroda introduced the term '''Kenmitsu (顕密)'''—which at the time referred to all of Buddhism—as a conceptual framework and proposed the '''Kenmitsu System Theory (顕密体制論)'''.
According to Kuroda, the '''orthodox religion''' in medieval Japan was '''Kenmitsu Buddhism''', which had been integrated '''around {{ill|Mikkyō|en|Mikkyō|WD=Q336102|lt=Esoteric Buddhism|1=Mikkyō}}''' (密教) since the '''{{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}'''. The Eight Traditional Buddhist Schools were '''not separate entities''' but functioned within '''a competitive yet unified order''', based on '''a common foundation of esoteric rituals and mystical faiths focused on pacification and incantations'''. Within this system, Esoteric Buddhism (密教) was considered the '''absolute and universal truth'''. Temples of the '''old Buddhist schools''', such as {{ill|Enryaku-ji|en|Enryaku-ji|WD=Q917195|lt=Enryaku-ji|1=Enryaku-ji}}, maintained '''close ties to state power''', solidifying their position as '''the legitimate religious institutions''' throughout the medieval period. This state of affairs is what Kuroda termed the '''Kenmitsu System'''.
This theory challenged the '''postwar perception''' that saw the {{ill|Kamakura Buddhism|lt=Kamakura Buddhism|lt=Kamakura New Buddhism|ja|鎌倉新仏教|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura New Buddhism|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鎌倉仏教}} '''(鎌倉新仏教)''' as the '''main representative of medieval Buddhism''', instead emphasizing that '''the old Buddhist schools were the true mainstream of medieval Japanese Buddhism'''. Additionally, by incorporating '''Buddhism into the broader framework of political and social history''', Kuroda's theory—built upon his earlier '''{{ill|Kenmon System Theory|ja|権門体制論|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmon System Theory|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 権門体制}} (権門体制論)'''—introduced '''a new perspective on Buddhist history'''.
